title: ADR 0008 — /api/categorize was the unguarded live amount boundary
content: ai-bookkeeper: ADR 0001's amount validator (parseAmount/isValidSignedAmount, MAX_AMOUNT=$1M, 2dp, signed +income/-expense) is implemented but was wired to NOTHING but tests. The real untrusted-amount entry point is POST /api/categorize (src/app/api/categorize/route.ts) — its parseRequest gated on Number.isFinite only, letting through zero, >$1M, and sub-cent amounts. CategorizeRequest.amount is SIGNED, so isValidSignedAmount is the correct drop-in guard (parseAmount expects an unsigned magnitude+direction). ADR 0008 (Accepted 2026-05-31) scopes the wiring as one 2pt engineer task; extends ADR 0001. Lesson for future reviews: when a validator exists, grep for its callers — an unwired validator is a silent gap. Future-work (NOT this run): route-level currency-symbol string parsing; integer-cents migration still deferred to the persistence/Cloud SQL ADR.

title: ai-paralegal design DNA = "Capital Chambers" (parchment/walnut/oxblood/brass)
content: ai-paralegal's visual identity (tailwind.config.ts + globals.css + layout.tsx, visual v2 'Capital Chambers') is a paper-and-seal legal-chambers system. Four-role palette: NEUTRAL parchment #f6efe3 / vellum #fbf6ec (canvas 80-90%); PRIMARY walnut-black ink #1c1410; SECONDARY brass #9c7a2b (signed/approved/sealed) + ink-mute #7b6852; TERTIARY oxblood #6b1620 (the wax-seal CTA, used least). Fonts: Fraunces (display/headline), Newsreader (body 16-18px), JetBrains Mono caps 0.28em tracking (labels/stamps). Sensory vocabulary to reuse in prompts: parchment ground, walnut ink pressed into page, oxblood wax seal, brass gilt edge, foxed hairline edges, engraved letterhead, impressed desk shadows (no glow), 2-3px cut-from-a-sheet corners. Negative-prompt fragments: no pure black, no cold ivory/blue-white, no neon/glow, no rounded pill content cards, no sans-serif body. Use these verbatim instead of generic adjectives in any future asset prompt.

title: Session capture (Stop) — 2026-05-31 18:44 UTC
Capital Chambers asset prompts must explicitly force a visible oxblood wax seal
content: When generating ai-paralegal 'Capital Chambers' assets via Leonardo (Phoenix model), the TERTIARY oxblood #6b1620 wax-seal accent consistently fails to render — both cycle-1 variations of the hero asset scored well on parchment/walnut/brass but vision eval flagged 'oxblood missing entirely.' The phrase 'used sparingly' appears to cause the model to drop it. Future prompts should foreground the accent (e.g. 'a prominent, clearly visible deep oxblood (#6b1620) wax seal in the lower-right, glossy impressed wax texture') rather than describing it as minimal. Parchment ground, walnut ink, brass gilt, foxed edges, engraved letterpress, and notch-corner/no-glow style all render reliably and scored 8-9 on style consistency.

title: Session capture (Stop) — 2026-05-31 18:47 UTC
ai-bookkeeper: route handlers can't be imported in tsx --test (server-only) — extract pure logic to test it
content: When unit-testing logic that lives inside a Next.js route handler in ai-bookkeeper, you CANNOT import the route module from a node:test/`tsx --test` file: importing `src/app/api/.../route.ts` transitively pulls `@/lib/ai` → the `server-only` package, which is unresolvable outside the Next bundler and throws ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND. Pattern that works: extract the pure, dependency-light function (e.g. request validation/coercion) into a sibling module (e.g. `parse-request.ts`) importing only `@/features/ledger` + type-only `@/lib/categorize`; the route imports it, and the test imports the same module — single source of truth, fully testable. The `@/features/ledger` barrel itself resolves fine under tsx (confirmed). Applied for ADR 0008 (commit 326ce2c). Lesson for future Architect handoffs: a 'just export X from the route and test it' plan is not viable for server-only routes — budget for an extraction.

title: Session capture (Stop) — 2026-05-31 18:52 UTC
ai-bookkeeper categorize boundary: description is unbounded + unescaped into the LLM prompt (injection + cost risk)
content: At the POST /api/categorize ingestion boundary, parseRequest (src/app/api/categorize/parse-request.ts) trims the description but never caps its length, and src/lib/categorize.ts buildPrompt interpolates that raw string directly into the model's instruction block with no delimiter or escaping. Two consequences: (1) unbounded prompt size → token-cost blowup on a metered op; (2) a prompt-injection surface — attacker-controlled description text becomes part of the model instructions. Pre-existing (not introduced by commit 326ce2c / ADR 0008 T1, which only tightened the *amount* gate). Recommended fix: cap description length in parseRequest (~500 chars) and wrap it in a delimited block in buildPrompt. The amount path has a secondary low-sev issue: Number() coercion accepts hex/binary/exponent strings ('0x10'->16) — should be regex-gated to plain decimals.

title: Session capture (Stop) — 2026-05-31 18:55 UTC
ai-bookkeeper: LLM prompt boundaries need both a length cap AND delimiting
content: At ai-bookkeeper's POST /api/categorize, free-text user input (`description`) flows into a metered LLM prompt via buildPrompt (categorize.ts). Any such boundary carries TWO distinct risks that need TWO distinct mitigations: (1) token-cost/DoS — mitigated by a length cap enforced in the request validator (parse-request.ts), consistent with the ADR 0008 reject-don't-coerce pattern; (2) prompt injection — mitigated by wrapping the value in an explicit delimiter with a 'treat as data, not instructions' guard line in the prompt builder. Capping alone does not stop injection; delimiting alone does not stop cost abuse. Treat this as a checklist for any future user-text→prompt path in this codebase.

title: Session capture (Stop) — 2026-05-31 18:57 UTC
ai-bookkeeper coverage: polar/webhook ledger logic extracted + tested (next: checkout)
content: Covered the Polar webhook (src/app/api/polar/webhook) this run by extracting its event→ledger decision into a pure sibling resolve-order.ts (resolveCreditAction) and reducing route.ts to a thin shell — the parse-request.ts pattern, required because route handlers pull server-only and can't import under tsx --test. 9 tests pin purchase credit vs refund clawback (amount sign, reason, idempotency ref refund:, bundle resolution). Suite 151→160, tsc+eslint clean. Reusable lesson: BUNDLES polarProductId is read from env at module-load, so the product-id-fallback branch of bundle resolution is NOT unit-testable without dynamic import — test via the env-independent metadata.bundle key path instead. API-route coverage now 2/9; next highest-risk extractable target is checkout/route.ts (unknown_bundle 400 + auth gating).
